entries.title: About the Course
entries.content:
Each year in High School, students read one of the gospels along with several other New Testament epistles. For 2026-2027 they read the gospel of Luke along with 1-2 Timothy, Titus, Philemon, James, 1-2 Peter, 1-3 John, and Jude. Using their study Bibles and other resources, students encounter scripture and seek to understand the cultural, historical, and theological context of their readings in order to more deeply appreciate the richness of the Bible and the humility needed in order to live out God's word today.

This topic is included in the following course(s): Bible: Grades 10-11

entries.title: Placement & Combining Tips
entries.content: Lessons in one of the gospels are shared across HS grades. The epistles scheduled in Grades 10-11 are a little more thematically challenging than those in Grade 9, but lessons may be combined with Grade 9 or Grade 12 if desired. Students new to reading the Bible for themselves or unused to commentaries or study notes may find the lessons in Grades 7-8 a gentler introduction to these study practices.

rows.label: New Testament: Grades 10-11
rows.books: NIV Cultural Backgrounds Study Bible: Bringing to Life the Ancient World of Scripture||Introducing the New Testament: A Historical, Literary, and Theological Survey||Luke for Everyone

entries.content:
Permission to print for non-commercial use. See Alveary group use policy to use lessons
in a group context.

LINKS: Click text or scan the QR code in the top corner of the lesson plan pages to view
online resources associated with the lessons.

Responsibility for previewing all links rests with the teacher. All links were checked at the
time of publication; however, websites change frequently and may contain objectionable
content. Please report broken links by contacting us through our website.

books.title: NIV Cultural Backgrounds Study Bible: Bringing to Life the Ancient World of Scripture
Introducing the New Testament: A Historical, Literary, and Theological Survey

blocks.text: Take a moment to recall what you read previously in the same book or topic.

Read the passage assigned or read for the number of minutes allocated. If a book is challenging for you, try stopping to narrate to yourself silently now and then to make sure you are tracking. Or you could listen to an audiobook version of the text and read along. You may also want to read aloud to yourself. If a book is an easy read, enjoy the ride! Keep an eye out for quotes to put in your Commonplace Book or events or characters to put down in your Book of Centuries.

Process and integrate your reading by narrating either orally or in writing. Some lesson plans will give you a suggestion of how best to narrate. You might want to set a timer to keep yourself on track, so you stop reading with enough time to narrate.

Read notes in your Study Bible or commentary. Look up places mentioned in the text in your Bible atlas. Add something to your Book of Centuries or Commonplace Book. Jot down additional reflections and thoughts in your Bible notebook or share with others.

Read over the lesson plans to familiarize yourself with the content for accountability and interest. If doing the lessons together, use a picture, a map, or an engaging question to rouse students' interest before reading. You may also highlight one or two unfamiliar terms if needed. If in a classroom context, you may choose to leave larger doctrinal conversations for students to have with their parents at home.

lessons.title: 30m New Testament: Grades 10-11 - Lesson 1
lessons.body:
➜ INTRO
Each year in HS we will be reading one of the Gospels along with our study of the rest of the NT books. This year we will be reading Matthew along with James, Philemon, 1,2 Timothy, Titus, 1,2 Peter, Jude, and 1,2,3 John. It is helpful to be continually reminded of who Jesus is and how he interacted with people. The Gospel accounts were designed to be read many times.  There will be insights gained that you would only pick up after multiple readings and by paying attention to repeated words and themes. Alongside our study of Matthew, we will read the First Nations Version of Matthew.  Normally, when people translate the Bible for another culture, the text is not in English.  In this case, the text is in English, so we can join in!  Reading Matthew through the eyes of First Nations people helps us to see the gospel stories from a different perspective. It also gives us insights into the culture of the First Nations people. If you find you do not have time in the lesson for this reading, consider using it as your devotional reading.

We will make use of the Cultural Backgrounds Study Bible notes, the atlas, and some Bible Project videos/articles/podcasts to help us understand what Matthew and Paul are saying to their audiences.  Then we can think about what new thoughts about God we gain in the process.  You do not need to read all the study notes for all the verses.  Pick and choose based on which verses you have questions about. Use a digital or physical notebook to record your thoughts as you work your way through the readings. 

➜ VIEW & NARRATE
Today we will set the stage for reading Matthew by gaining some context. Narrate silently in your head or orally to yourself by going over the main points. Or, jot them down in your journal.
∞ Video Link: How to Read the New Testament
∞ Video Link: How to Read the Gospel
∞ Video Link: Matthew 1-13 Summary

➜ PRINT
You may want to print out the summary of the book to reference as you read even though it includes the second part of Matthew that we will watch when we get to those chapters.
∞ Image Link: Summary of Matthew Poster

➜ MAPWORK
When places are named in the text refer to a map in the study Bible or use your atlas.  The maps in the atlas that relate to Matthew are found on p.303-333.  Finding the places on the map is very helpful for seeing the stories in the context of the land.  Sketching maps of journeys or regions can be helpful too. The atlas will always be mentioned in the materials so that you always have it nearby.
lessons.teacherNotes:
Note:
This journal option contains only the Bible book text and space to write.
∞ Link: ESV Scripture Journal Matthew

lessons.title: 30m New Testament: Grades 10-11 - Lesson 2
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Cultural Backgrounds Study Bible, First Nations Version, Ultimate Bible Atlas 

➜ READ  
Matthew 1-3

➜ NARRATE & RESPOND
Silently or orally tell back to yourself what you read.  Then in your journal, respond to what you read by putting down some of your thoughts or questions.  Just like in nature study when we ask questions such as What did I notice?, What did I wonder?, or What did this remind me of?, we can ask the same questions about what we read.  

➜ READ & DISCUSS
∞ Article Link: Jesus and Genealogies

Scroll down below the video to read the article. Find someone to discuss the article with.  Did you know genealogies could communicate so much?

➜ READ & NOTE 
First Nations Version
Matthew 1-3
Note any interesting aspects that stand out about the text or the cultural aspects that come through.
lessons.teacherNotes:
Instead of reading Matthew for some of the lessons, consider watching and listening to Matthew via the LUMO Project which only uses the actual text of Matthew but has people acting while it is read. There are many versions and many languages available.  You might consider this resource for your modern language studies too.

∞ Video Link: Lumo Project Matthew

The Bible Project has an interesting podcast series on the Sermon on the Mount if you are interested in learning more!

∞ Podcast Series Link: Sermon on the Mount

Their app has a weekly playlist to go deeper into several topics for continued study.
∞ Info Link: Bible Project App

⍞ Materials:  Cultural Backgrounds Study Bible, First Nations Version, Chronological Guide, Ultimate Bible Atlas 

➜ VIEW
Today we will set the stage for reading James by gaining some context specific to the book.
∞ Video Link: Intro to James

➜ PRINT
You may want to print out the summary of the book to reference as you read James. 
∞ Image Link: Summary of James Poster

➜ READ
This guide might be helpful to refer to as you read James.  It also has suggestions for further study.
∞ Article Link: Book of James Guide

➜ READ, NARRATE, & RESPOND
James 1-2 
Skip any articles for now, so you can focus on the text. 

➜ READ
Any of the verse commentary under the text for verses that might need more explanation.  Pick and choose.  You do not need to read all of it unless you want to.

➜ EXPLORE 
As you have time left in the lesson or on your own: 
• First Nations Version (today's reading)
       Note any differences that stand out to you. 
• Skipped-over articles in your Cultural Backgrounds Study Bible 
• Chronological Guide (p.188) 
• Ultimate Atlas (p.334-364)
